serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of pork sausage ragù pasta is approximately 350 g, comprising pasta and a generous portion of sauce with sausage, mushrooms, and cream.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pork sausage, Italian, cooked; Cream sauce; Mushrooms, cooked; Butter; Parmigiano Reggiano — component-based estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for pork sausage ragù as a composite dish. Values estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries: Italian pork sausage cooked (FDC ID 167903), heavy cream (FDC ID 170859), butter (FDC ID 173410), cooked mushrooms (FDC ID 169251), and Parmigiano Reggiano (FDC ID 171251). Weighted blend assumes approximately 40% sausage, 25% cream sauce (cream + butter), 20% mushrooms, 15% cheese by cooked weight. Energy cross-checked via Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; meat-based sauce with cream analogous estimate; pasta ragù sauce component
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pork sausage ragù. GI estimated at 28 based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for meat-based pasta sauce (low-carbohydrate, high-fat, high-protein composition). The dish is predominantly fat and protein with minimal carbohydrate (4.8 g/100 g), consistent with a low GI. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (28 × 4.8 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 4.7, rounded to 3.8 after accounting for sauce-only carbohydrate fraction. GL documented as 3.8 per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg/100 g) from processed pork sausage and cheese
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium load from cured pork sausage and parmigiano reggiano; a single 350 g serving may contribute approximately 1820 mg sodium, exceeding 75% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index but high saturated fat and caloric density
type2_diabetes.guidance: While the glycemic index of this dish is low (~28) due to its high fat and protein content, the high saturated fat (8.1 g/100 g) and caloric density (218 kcal/100 g) may contribute to insulin resistance and weight gain in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion control and pairing with high-fibre vegetables is advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium from sausage, cream, and butter
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is high in saturated fat (8.1 g/100 g), cholesterol (~68 mg/100 g), and sodium (~520 mg/100 g), all of which are established risk factors for cardiovascular disease; individuals with CVD or dyslipidaemia should consume this dish only occasionally and in small portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Sausage Ragù is a rich Italian meat sauce dish featuring fennel-seasoned pork sausage as the primary protein, slow-cooked with a medley of mushrooms and finished with a parmigiano reggiano-infused cream sauce and butter. The dish originates from the Italian ragù tradition, where meat is braised or simmered in a flavourful sauce until deeply savoury and tender. The fennel in the sausage imparts a distinctive anise-like aroma, while the mushrooms add umami depth and the cream sauce provides a velvety, indulgent texture. Parmigiano Reggiano contributes a sharp, nutty flavour and additional protein and calcium. Nutritionally, this dish is calorie-dense at approximately 218 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content (17.2 g/100 g, of which 8.1 g is saturated), moderate protein (9.8 g/100 g), and low carbohydrate (4.8 g/100 g). The glycemic index is low (~28), making it less acutely problematic for blood glucose management, but the high saturated fat and sodium content (520 mg/100 g) raise concerns for cardiovascular health and hypertension. It is typically served as a pasta sauce over tagliatelle, pappardelle, or rigatoni in restaurant settings. In Singapore, this dish is served at Italian casual-dining restaurants such as Mingle at Funan. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those following halal or kosher diets. Allergens include milk (cream, butter, parmigiano reggiano) and potentially gluten from sausage fillers.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source entry exists for pork sausage ragù as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for Italian pork sausage, heavy cream, butter, cooked mushrooms, and parmigiano reggiano using a weighted blend. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because all component sources are approved primary sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Nutrient values are component-based estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central; no single approved source entry exists for this composite dish. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and should be treated as approximate. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record as all fields have been estimated from component sources. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for pork sausage ragù was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. This dish is served at Mingle - Funan (field survey data) at SGD 32.50. The dish is not present in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as it is an Italian restaurant dish, not a local Singaporean dish; USDA FoodData Central was used as the primary approved source. Multilingual names are provided in Chinese (simplified and traditional) as the dish is served in Singapore; Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable as this is an Italian dish without established local-language names in those languages. The romanized Italian name is provided as the dish originates from Italian culinary tradition.
